abstract:
Recent advancements in generative models have enabled high-fidelity
text-to-image generation. However, open-source image-editing models still lag
behind their proprietary counterparts, primarily due to limited high-quality
data and insufficient benchmarks. To overcome these limitations, we introduce
ImgEdit, a large-scale, high-quality image-editing dataset comprising 1.2
million carefully curated edit pairs, which contain both novel and complex
single-turn edits, as well as challenging multi-turn tasks. To ensure the data
quality, we employ a multi-stage pipeline that integrates a cutting-edge
vision-language model, a detection model, a segmentation model, alongside
task-specific in-painting procedures and strict post-processing. ImgEdit
surpasses existing datasets in both task novelty and data quality. Using
ImgEdit, we train ImgEdit-E1, an editing model using Vision Language Model to
process the reference image and editing prompt, which outperforms existing
open-source models on multiple tasks, highlighting the value of ImgEdit and
model design. For comprehensive evaluation, we introduce ImgEdit-Bench, a
benchmark designed to evaluate image editing performance in terms of
instruction adherence, editing quality, and detail preservation. It includes a
basic testsuite, a challenging single-turn suite, and a dedicated multi-turn
suite. We evaluate both open-source and proprietary models, as well as
ImgEdit-E1, providing deep analysis and actionable insights into the current
behavior of image-editing models. The source data are publicly available on
this https URL

keyInsights: High-quality data like ImgEdit is essential for advancing image editing capabilities, particularly for complex and identity-preserving edits.
Instruction understanding (enabled by VLM encoders), grounding ability (precise localization), and editing generalization across diverse operations are key factors determining model performance.
True multi-turn conversational image editing, including content memory and version backtracking, remains an underdeveloped area even for state-of-the-art closed-source models.
originalProblem: A significant performance gap exists between proprietary and open-source text-to-image editing models, primarily due to the scarcity of high-quality, publicly available datasets.

Existing open-source datasets suffer from sub-optimal quality (low resolution, simplistic prompts, inaccurate edits) and limited task coverage, often lacking support for complex scenarios like identity preservation or multi-object manipulation.
Current evaluation benchmarks are inadequate, lacking diverse and nuanced dimensions, failing to stratify task difficulty, and often relying on metrics that poorly correlate with human judgment.
results: The ImgEdit dataset, with 1.2 million high-quality image-editing pairs, achieved the highest quality score (4.71) compared to prior datasets and produced significantly more realistic edits with a much lower 'Fake score' (0.050).
ImgEdit-E1, trained on ImgEdit, consistently outperformed existing open-source models, especially excelling in novel tasks like identity-consistent object extraction and complex hybrid edits.

GPT-4o-Image consistently led on the ImgEdit-Bench, particularly in the challenging UGE suite, though all models, including GPT-4o-Image, showed high 'fake scores' and limited multi-turn conversational capabilities.
solution: Constructed ImgEdit, a large-scale, high-quality image editing dataset of 1.2 million pairs using a multi-stage automated pipeline involving GPT-4o for instruction generation and advanced generative models for in-painting.

Introduced ImgEdit-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for image editing evaluation featuring Basic-Edit, Understanding-Grounding-Editing (UGE), and Multi-Turn suites, evaluated across instruction adherence, editing quality, and detail preservation.
Trained ImgEdit-E1, an advanced editing model integrating a VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) and a DiT backbone (FLUX), to serve as a strong open-source baseline and validate the dataset's effectiveness.
paper_summary.summary: Peking University and Peng Cheng Laboratory developed ImgEdit, a 1.2-million-pair high-quality dataset and a comprehensive benchmark, to close the performance gap between open and closed-source image editing models. Their trained ImgEdit-E1 model demonstrates state-of-the-art performance among open-source systems, particularly in novel tasks like identity-consistent object extraction.

title: ImgEdit: A Unified Image Editing Dataset and Benchmark

abstract: 3D local editing of specified regions is crucial for game industry and robot interaction. Recent methods typically edit rendered multi-view images and then reconstruct 3D models, but they face challenges in precisely preserving unedited regions and overall coherence. Inspired by structured 3D generative models, we propose VoxHammer, a novel training-free approach that performs precise and coherent editing in 3D latent space. Given a 3D model, VoxHammer first predicts its inversion trajectory and obtains its inverted latents and key-value tokens at each timestep. Subsequently, in the denoising and editing phase, we replace the denoising features of preserved regions with the corresponding inverted latents and cached key-value tokens. By retaining these contextual features, this approach ensures consistent reconstruction of preserved areas and coherent integration of edited parts. To evaluate the consistency of preserved regions, we constructed Edit3D-Bench, a human-annotated dataset comprising hundreds of samples, each with carefully labeled 3D editing regions. Experiments demonstrate that VoxHammer significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of both 3D consistency of preserved regions and overall quality. Our method holds promise for synthesizing high-quality edited paired data, thereby laying the data foundation for in-context 3D generation. See our project page at this https URL.

keyInsights: Pretrained native 3D generative models can be effectively repurposed for precise local 3D editing without additional training by leveraging their latent space and attention mechanisms.
Accurate 3D inversion is critical for faithfully preserving unedited regions and maintaining overall 3D consistency throughout the editing process.
Replacing Key and Value tensors in self-attention layers within unedited regions, alongside latent blending, prevents 'concept leakage' and ensures strong contextual consistency.
originalProblem: Existing 3D editing methods, such as SDS-based approaches, suffer from slow processing times, often taking minutes to hours per scene.
Multi-view 2D image editing followed by 3D reconstruction frequently introduces inconsistencies, positional bias, and visual artifacts in the final 3D model.
Adapting advanced native 3D generative models for editing is challenging due to the scarcity of large-scale paired 3D local editing datasets and the high computational cost of fine-tuning.
results: VoxHammer demonstrated superior unedited region preservation, achieving the lowest Chamfer Distance (0.012) and highest masked PSNR (41.68) compared to baseline methods.
It produced higher overall 3D quality (FID 23.05, FVD 187.8) and better alignment with text prompts and edited images (DINO-I 0.947, CLIP-T 0.287).

A user study indicated a strong preference for VoxHammer, with 70.3% for text alignment and 81.2% for overall 3D quality, and the method generalized across various applications including NeRF and 3DGS editing.
solution: VoxHammer employs a two-phase, training-free framework: Precise 3D Inversion and Denoising & Editing, built upon a pretrained structured 3D latent diffusion model.
Precise 3D Inversion uses a Rectified Flow-inspired technique to accurately map an input 3D model back to its initial noise state, caching inverted latents and key-value tokens from attention layers.

Denoising and Editing performs local modifications by strategically blending edited region latents with inverted source latents and replacing key-value tensors in unedited regions with their cached counterparts during the denoising process, guided by a 3D mask and a 2D image diffusion model.
paper_summary.summary: VoxHammer offers a training-free framework for precise and coherent 3D local editing, operating directly within native 3D latent space. It achieves superior unedited region preservation, with a Chamfer Distance of 0.012 and a masked PSNR of 41.68, and also introduces the human-annotated Edit3D-Bench dataset for evaluating 3D editing performance.

title: VoxHammer: Training-Free Precise and Coherent 3D Editing in Native 3D Space

abstract: As user expectations for image editing continue to rise, the demand for flexible, fine-grained manipulation of specific visual elements presents a challenge for current diffusion-based methods. In this work, we present BlobCtrl, a framework for element-level image editing based on a probabilistic blob-based representation. Treating blobs as visual primitives, BlobCtrl disentangles layout from appearance, affording fine-grained, controllable object-level manipulation. Our key contributions are twofold: (1) an in-context dual-branch diffusion model that separates foreground and background processing, incorporating blob representations to explicitly decouple layout and appearance, and (2) a self-supervised disentangle-then-reconstruct training paradigm with an identity-preserving loss function, along with tailored strategies to efficiently leverage blob-image pairs. To foster further research, we introduce BlobData for large-scale training and BlobBench, a benchmark for systematic evaluation. Experimental results demonstrate that BlobCtrl achieves state-of-the-art performance in a variety of element-level editing tasks, such as object addition, removal, scaling, and replacement, while maintaining computational efficiency. Project Webpage: this https URL

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

title: BlobCtrl: Taming Controllable Blob for Element-level Image Editing

abstract: Significant progress has been made in the field of Instruction-based Image Editing (IIE). However, evaluating these models poses a significant challenge. A crucial requirement in this field is the establishment of a comprehensive evaluation benchmark for accurately assessing editing results and providing valuable insights for its further development. In response to this need, we propose I2EBench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to automatically evaluate the quality of edited images produced by IIE models from multiple dimensions. I2EBench consists of 2,000+ images for editing, along with 4,000+ corresponding original and diverse instructions. It offers three distinctive characteristics: 1) Comprehensive Evaluation Dimensions: I2EBench comprises 16 evaluation dimensions that cover both high-level and low-level aspects, providing a comprehensive assessment of each IIE model. 2) Human Perception Alignment: To ensure the alignment of our benchmark with human perception, we conducted an extensive user study for each evaluation dimension. 3) Valuable Research Insights: By analyzing the advantages and disadvantages of existing IIE models across the 16 dimensions, we offer valuable research insights to guide future development in the field. We will open-source I2EBench, including all instructions, input images, human annotations, edited images from all evaluated methods, and a simple script for evaluating the results from new IIE models. The code, dataset and generated images from all IIE models are provided in github: this https URL.

keyInsights: Existing IIE models demonstrate specialized strengths, with no single model consistently excelling across all 16 diverse evaluation dimensions.
I²EBench's automated evaluation correlates strongly with human perception, achieving Pearson coefficients from 0.75 to 0.99 across dimensions.
IIE models integrating LLMs/MLLMs for instruction comprehension show increased robustness to variations in user prompts.
originalProblem: The rapid progress in Instruction-based Image Editing (IIE) models lacks a corresponding comprehensive and robust evaluation framework.
Existing evaluation metrics (e.g., CLIP Score, PSNR, SSIM) are often insufficient, failing to capture the nuances across diverse editing tasks.
Current benchmarks are limited in scope, focus on specific editing types (e.g., mask-guided), or rely heavily on manual evaluation, hindering reproducibility and universality.
results: Evaluation of 8 state-of-the-art IIE models demonstrated diverse strengths, with models like MGIE achieving over 11-point lead in Deblurring, and others excelling in specific high-level edits.
Performance varies across content categories, with "Scenery" and "Global" edits consistently outperforming localized edits on "Animal," "Object," or "Human" elements.
The "I²EBench change rate" revealed significant performance fluctuations in models lacking advanced language understanding when faced with diverse instructions.
solution: Established I²EBench, a comprehensive benchmark with 16 fine-grained evaluation dimensions covering both high-level semantic and low-level global editing tasks.
Utilizes Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) like GPT-4V for automated assessment of high-level editing quality and SSIM for low-level tasks, reducing reliance on manual evaluation.
Developed a diverse dataset of over 2,000 images and 4,000 instructions, including original and ChatGPT-generated diverse instructions for robustness testing.
paper_summary.summary: I²EBench introduces a comprehensive, multi-dimensional benchmark for evaluating instruction-based image editing models, featuring 16 fine-grained dimensions and leveraging Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for high-level assessment and SSIM for low-level tasks. This benchmark demonstrates strong alignment with human perception, achieving Pearson correlation coefficients of 0.75 to 0.99 across dimensions when assessing 8 state-of-the-art IIE models.

title: I2EBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Instruction-based Image Editing

abstract: We present BlenderFusion, a generative visual compositing framework that synthesizes new scenes by recomposing objects, camera, and background. It follows a layering-editing-compositing pipeline: (i) segmenting and converting visual inputs into editable 3D entities (layering), (ii) editing them in Blender with 3D-grounded control (editing), and (iii) fusing them into a coherent scene using a generative compositor (compositing). Our generative compositor extends a pre-trained diffusion model to process both the original (source) and edited (target) scenes in parallel. It is fine-tuned on video frames with two key training strategies: (i) source masking, enabling flexible modifications like background replacement; (ii) simulated object jittering, facilitating disentangled control over objects and camera. BlenderFusion significantly outperforms prior methods in complex compositional scene editing tasks.

keyInsights: Integrating data-driven generative models with symbolic 3D graphics software enables precise, 3D-aware control over visual content that goes beyond text prompts.

Training strategies like source masking and simulated object jittering are effective for teaching generative models disentangled 3D control from existing video datasets, overcoming inherent data limitations.
By explicitly lifting 2D elements into a 3D space for manipulation and then reprojecting for generative rendering, the framework achieves fine-grained control over objects, camera, and background.
originalProblem: Current generative AI models lack precise, 3D-aware control over individual scene elements, making complex geometric manipulations challenging.
Existing 3D-aware editing methods are often limited to single objects, coarse control, or computationally expensive per-scene optimizations.
Achieving disentangled control over objects, camera, and background is difficult, as current models often entangle these elements leading to unintended changes.
results: BlenderFusion consistently outperforms prior methods like Object 3DIT and Neural Assets across quantitative metrics (PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, FID), showing improved object appearance preservation and overall photorealism.

It demonstrates precise disentangled control, accurately following 3D transformations for objects (translation, rotation, scaling) while maintaining object identity and stable backgrounds with fixed cameras.
Human evaluations indicate a strong preference for BlenderFusion's outputs, with 87.04% overall preference, especially for complex, fine-grained compositional edits.
solution: A "layering-editing-compositing" pipeline integrates generative models with Blender for 3D-grounded visual editing.
Input images are transformed into editable 3D meshes using segmentation and depth estimation (e.g., SAM2, Depth Pro), then imported into Blender for precise manipulations.

A dual-stream diffusion model, adapted from Stable Diffusion, performs generative compositing by blending coarse Blender renderings with context from the original image to produce photorealistic outputs.
paper_summary.summary: BlenderFusion is a framework from Google DeepMind that integrates generative models with 3D graphics software like Blender to enable precise, 3D-aware visual editing and generative compositing. It achieves fine-grained, disentangled control over objects, camera, and background, yielding higher fidelity outputs compared to previous methods, with a human preference of 87.04%.

title: BlenderFusion: 3D-Grounded Visual Editing and Generative Compositing

abstract: Drag-based editing allows precise object manipulation through point-based control, offering user convenience. However, current methods often suffer from a geometric inconsistency problem by focusing exclusively on matching user-defined points, neglecting the broader geometry and leading to artifacts or unstable edits. We propose FlowDrag, which leverages geometric information for more accurate and coherent transformations. Our approach constructs a 3D mesh from the image, using an energy function to guide mesh deformation based on user-defined drag points. The resulting mesh displacements are projected into 2D and incorporated into a UNet denoising process, enabling precise handle-to-target point alignment while preserving structural integrity. Additionally, existing drag-editing benchmarks provide no ground truth, making it difficult to assess how accurately the edits match the intended transformations. To address this, we present VFD (VidFrameDrag) benchmark dataset, which provides ground-truth frames using consecutive shots in a video dataset. FlowDrag outperforms existing drag-based editing methods on both VFD Bench and DragBench.
title: FlowDrag: 3D-aware Drag-based Image Editing with Mesh-guided Deformation Vector Flow Fields

abstract:
We introduce MotionEdit, a novel dataset for motion-centric image editing-the task of modifying subject actions and interactions while preserving identity, structure, and physical plausibility. Unlike existing image editing datasets that focus on static appearance changes or contain only sparse, low-quality motion edits, MotionEdit provides high-fidelity image pairs depicting realistic motion transformations extracted and verified from continuous videos. This new task is not only scientifically challenging but also practically significant, powering downstream applications such as frame-controlled video synthesis and animation.
To evaluate model performance on the novel task, we introduce MotionEdit-Bench, a benchmark that challenges models on motion-centric edits and measures model performance with generative, discriminative, and preference-based metrics. Benchmark results reveal that motion editing remains highly challenging for existing state-of-the-art diffusion-based editing models. To address this gap, we propose MotionNFT (Motion-guided Negative-aware Fine Tuning), a post-training framework that computes motion alignment rewards based on how well the motion flow between input and model-edited images matches the ground-truth motion, guiding models toward accurate motion transformations. Extensive experiments on FLUX.1 Kontext and Qwen-Image-Edit show that MotionNFT consistently improves editing quality and motion fidelity of both base models on the motion editing task without sacrificing general editing ability, demonstrating its effectiveness. Our code is at this https URL.

keyInsights: Existing image editing models exhibit substantial limitations in executing complex motion edits due to a lack of specialized high-quality data and explicit motion-aware guidance.
Incorporating an explicit optical flow-based motion reward is critical for training generative models to accurately interpret and perform fine-grained kinematic transformations.
A hybrid reward system, balancing geometric understanding (optical flow) with semantic comprehension (MLLM), is most effective for achieving precise and plausible motion image editing.
originalProblem: Existing instruction-guided image editing models and datasets primarily focus on static, appearance-only modifications, overlooking dynamic aspects like motion, posture, or interaction.
Current datasets that do include motion edits are often small-scale, feature low data quality, and contain unfaithful or incoherent ground truths, hindering robust model training and evaluation.

State-of-the-art image editing models struggle with 'editing inertia' and 'motion misalignment', frequently failing to accurately translate motion-related natural language instructions into coherent spatial changes.
results: MotionNFT substantially improved the MLLM-rated Overall quality score, boosting FLUX.1 Kontext from 3.84 to 4.25 (+10.68%) and Qwen-Image-Edit from 4.65 to 4.72.
The framework significantly enhanced Motion Alignment Scores (MAS) for base models, with FLUX.1 Kontext increasing from 53.73 to 55.45 and Qwen-Image-Edit from 56.46 to 57.23.

Ablation studies confirmed that MotionNFT preserves general editing capabilities and that the explicit optical flow-based motion reward is crucial, outperforming MLLM-only reward training in motion alignment.
solution: The authors created MotionEdit, a video-driven dataset comprising 10,157 high-fidelity motion-editable frame pairs, ensuring realistic and coherent motion transitions with significant pixel-level displacement.

They proposed MotionNFT (Motion-guided Negative-aware FineTuning), a post-training framework extending DiffusionNFT with a novel optical flow-based motion-centric reward signal to quantify intended action accuracy.

MotionNFT employs a multi-score reward formulation, combining 50% of the optical flow-based Motion Reward with 50% of an MLLM-based semantic reward to leverage both geometric and semantic guidance during training.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces MotionEdit, a high-quality dataset and benchmark specifically for motion-centric image editing, alongside MotionNFT, a learning framework that integrates optical flow-based rewards to guide diffusion models. MotionNFT enhanced the MLLM-rated overall quality score of FLUX.1 Kontext by 10.68% and improved motion alignment scores for both FLUX.1 Kontext and Qwen-Image-Edit.

title: MotionEdit: Benchmarking and Learning Motion-Centric Image Editing

abstract: We tackle the task of geometric image editing, where an object within an image is repositioned, reoriented, or reshaped while preserving overall scene coherence. Previous diffusion-based editing methods often attempt to handle all relevant subtasks in a single step, proving difficult when transformations become large or structurally complex. We address this by proposing a decoupled pipeline that separates object transformation, source region inpainting, and target region refinement. Both inpainting and refinement are implemented using a training-free diffusion approach, FreeFine. In experiments on our new GeoBench benchmark, which contains both 2D and 3D editing scenarios, FreeFine outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives in image fidelity, and edit precision, especially under demanding transformations. Code and benchmark are available at: this https URL

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Decomposing complex geometric editing into distinct, manageable subtasks can effectively mitigate conflicting demands and lead to more robust, artifact-free results, especially for large transformations.

High-fidelity image editing, including complex structural completion, can be achieved without task-specific training by intelligently leveraging the powerful generative priors of pre-trained diffusion models through controlled latent space manipulation.

Dynamic blending of attention, localized stochasticity, and content-specific textual guidance within the diffusion process are crucial for precise, context-aware, and user-controlled content generation and refinement.
originalProblem: Existing diffusion-based image editing methods, especially those with unified optimization objectives, struggle with large or geometrically complex transformations, frequently introducing artifacts.
Balancing conflicting demands of object manipulation, background infill, and object blending within a single editing framework leads to suboptimal results and visual inconsistencies.
The absence of a standardized, comprehensive benchmark for geometric image editing hinders systematic evaluation and comparison of methods across diverse scenarios and complexities.
results: FreeFine consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across all quantitative metrics (image quality, consistency, editing effectiveness) and scenarios (2D, 3D, structural completion) on the GeoBench benchmark.

Qualitative comparisons demonstrate FreeFine's ability to produce high-fidelity, artifact-free images and handle complex 3D rotations effectively, where competing methods often fail or generate significant artifacts.

A user study involving 35 participants showed a strong preference for FreeFine, with 70.2% for 2D edits and 88.8% for 3D edits, validating its practical effectiveness and superior realism compared to other approaches.
solution: A decoupled pipeline is proposed that sequentially addresses object transformation, source region inpainting, and target region refinement, alleviating competing optimization demands.

FreeFine, a training-free diffusion-based module, is developed for high-quality inpainting and refinement, utilizing Temporal Contextual Attention, Local Perturbation, and Content-specified Generation.

The GeoBench benchmark is introduced, featuring 811 source images and 5,988 editing instructions for diverse 2D and 3D geometric edits, including structural completion tasks, to enable rigorous evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: This work introduces a decoupled pipeline for geometric image editing that separates object transformation, source region inpainting, and target region refinement. Its training-free diffusion-based module, FreeFine, leverages novel attention, perturbation, and content generation techniques to produce high-fidelity, artifact-free edits, consistently outperforming existing methods across 2D, 3D, and structural completion tasks on the new GeoBench benchmark.

title: Training-free Geometric Image Editing on Diffusion Models

abstract: We present FireRed-Image-Edit, a diffusion transformer for instruction-based image editing that achieves state-of-the-art performance through systematic optimization of data curation, training methodology, and evaluation design. We construct a 1.6B-sample training corpus, comprising 900M text-to-image and 700M image editing pairs from diverse sources. After rigorous cleaning, stratification, auto-labeling, and two-stage filtering, we retain over 100M high-quality samples balanced between generation and editing, ensuring strong semantic coverage and instruction alignment. Our multi-stage training pipeline progressively builds editing capability via pre-training, supervised fine-tuning, and reinforcement learning. To improve data efficiency, we introduce a Multi-Condition Aware Bucket Sampler for variable-resolution batching and Stochastic Instruction Alignment with dynamic prompt re-indexing. To stabilize optimization and enhance controllability, we propose Asymmetric Gradient Optimization for DPO, DiffusionNFT with layout-aware OCR rewards for text editing, and a differentiable Consistency Loss for identity preservation. We further establish REDEdit-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark spanning 15 editing categories, including newly introduced beautification and low-level enhancement tasks. Extensive experiments on REDEdit-Bench and public benchmarks (ImgEdit and GEdit) demonstrate competitive or superior performance against both open-source and proprietary systems. We release code, models, and the benchmark suite to support future research.

keyInsights: Superior instruction-based image editing performance can be achieved through systematic optimization across data, training, and evaluation, rather than solely through brute-force scaling of model parameters.

Meticulously curated, high-quality, and diverse datasets, generated through sophisticated auto-labeling and multi-strategy production engines, are crucial for training robust and controllable generative models.

Comprehensive, multi-dimensional benchmarks that align academic assessment with practical utility and human perception, incorporating task-specific metrics like OCR for text editing, are essential for guiding model development.
originalProblem: A dichotomy exists between highly performant, opaque commercial image editing systems and open-source models often requiring unsustainable computational scaling.
The efficient construction of high-quality, instruction-aligned datasets for complex image editing tasks remains a significant challenge.

Current evaluation metrics and benchmarks often fail to capture the nuanced requirements of production-ready applications, leading to a disconnect between academic scores and real-world user experience.
results: FireRed-Image-Edit achieved the highest human evaluation scores for consistency preservation among all compared methods and demonstrated competitive prompt following against commercial systems.
The model attained state-of-the-art performance on ImgEdit (overall score 4.56), GEdit (7.943 EN, 7.887 CN), and the new REDEdit-Bench (Overall 4.26 EN, 4.33 CN) across various general editing tasks.

Strong capabilities were shown in text-centric editing on REDEdit-Bench (OCR score 0.983, SuccessEdit 9.57) and superior fidelity in creative and virtual try-on tasks, outperforming baselines in legibility, style consistency, and detail preservation.
solution: An end-to-end framework, built around a Double-Stream Multi-Modal Diffusion Transformer (MM-DiT), systematically optimizes data curation, training methodology, and evaluation design.
A multi-stage data curation pipeline constructs a 1.6 billion-sample corpus, employing rigorous filtering, multi-strategy data production, long-tail supplementation, and multi-scale captioning.

Advanced training techniques are implemented, including Multi-Condition Aware Bucket Sampler, Stochastic Instruction Alignment, Asymmetric Gradient Optimization for DPO, DiffusionNFT with VLM and OCR rewards, and a differentiable Consistency Loss.
paper_summary.summary: Xiaohongshu Inc.'s Super Intelligence Team developed FireRed-Image-Edit-1.0, an instruction-based image editing model that achieves competitive performance against both open-source and commercial systems by systematically optimizing data, training, and evaluation rather than solely relying on large model parameters. The model demonstrates superior prompt following and consistency preservation across general, text-centric, creative, and try-on editing tasks.

title: FireRed-Image-Edit-1.0 Techinical Report

abstract: While real-world applications increasingly demand intricate scene manipulation, existing instruction-guided image editing benchmarks often oversimplify task complexity and lack comprehensive, fine-grained instructions. To bridge this gap, we introduce CompBench, a large-scale benchmark specifically designed for complex instruction-guided image editing. CompBench features challenging editing scenarios that incorporate fine-grained instruction following, spatial and contextual reasoning, thereby enabling comprehensive evaluation of image editing models' precise manipulation capabilities. To construct CompBench, we propose an MLLM-human collaborative framework with tailored task pipelines. Furthermore, we propose an instruction decoupling strategy that disentangles editing intents into four key dimensions: location, appearance, dynamics, and objects, ensuring closer alignment between instructions and complex editing requirements. Extensive evaluations reveal that CompBench exposes fundamental limitations of current image editing models and provides critical insights for the development of next-generation instruction-guided image editing systems. Our project page is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China
The Chinese University of Hong Kong

title: CompBench: Benchmarking Complex Instruction-guided Image Editing

abstract: We present DanceText, a training-free framework for multilingual text editing in images, designed to support complex geometric transformations and achieve seamless foreground-background integration. While diffusion-based generative models have shown promise in text-guided image synthesis, they often lack controllability and fail to preserve layout consistency under non-trivial manipulations such as rotation, translation, scaling, and warping. To address these limitations, DanceText introduces a layered editing strategy that separates text from the background, allowing geometric transformations to be performed in a modular and controllable manner. A depth-aware module is further proposed to align appearance and perspective between the transformed text and the reconstructed background, enhancing photorealism and spatial consistency. Importantly, DanceText adopts a fully training-free design by integrating pretrained modules, allowing flexible deployment without task-specific fine-tuning. Extensive experiments on the AnyWord-3M benchmark demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance in visual quality, especially under large-scale and complex transformation scenarios. Code is avaible at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Kunming University of Science and Technology

title: DanceText: A Training-Free Layered Framework for Controllable Multilingual Text Transformation in Images

abstract: Recent advances in image editing have enabled models to handle complex instructions with impressive realism. However, existing evaluation frameworks lag behind: current benchmarks suffer from narrow task coverage, while standard metrics fail to adequately capture visual consistency, i.e., the preservation of identity, structure and semantic coherence between edited and original images. To address these limitations, we introduce GEditBench v2, a comprehensive benchmark with 1,200 real-world user queries spanning 23 tasks, including a dedicated open-set category for unconstrained, out-of-distribution editing instructions beyond predefined tasks. Furthermore, we propose PVC-Judge, an open-source pairwise assessment model for visual consistency, trained via two novel region-decoupled preference data synthesis pipelines. Besides, we construct VCReward-Bench using expert-annotated preference pairs to assess the alignment of PVC-Judge with human judgments on visual consistency evaluation. Experiments show that our PVC-Judge achieves state-of-the-art evaluation performance among open-source models and even surpasses GPT-5.1 on average. Finally, by benchmarking 16 frontier editing models, we show that GEditBench v2 enables more human-aligned evaluation, revealing critical limitations of current models, and providing a reliable foundation for advancing precise image editing.

keyInsights: An inherent trade-off exists between aggressive instruction execution and maintaining visual consistency, leading to an 'under-editing' trap where models achieve high consistency by making minimal changes.

Current open-source models struggle with interpreting implicit user intentions in open-set instructions, complex inter-object relations, and preserving fidelity in fine-grained details like small faces.

The pairwise VLM-as-a-Judge paradigm, particularly with a specialized model like PVC-Judge, demonstrates stronger alignment with human preferences compared to pointwise scoring or general-purpose VLMs.
originalProblem: Existing image editing benchmarks have narrow task coverage, failing to assess model generalization across unconstrained, real-world scenarios.
Current evaluation metrics often inadequately capture visual consistency, which is critical for high-quality image edits.
VLM-as-a-Judge paradigms face limitations such as reliance on proprietary APIs, accuracy-cost trade-offs for open-source alternatives, and poor alignment of pointwise scoring with human judgment.
results: PVC-Judge achieved 81.82% average accuracy on VCReward-Bench, outperforming GPT-5.1 (76.89%) and existing reward models in visual consistency assessment.
Overall Elo scores from GEditBench v2 showed a strong Spearman's rank correlation (ρ=0.929) with human-annotated Arena rankings, confirming the human alignment of the automated evaluation.
Benchmarking 16 frontier models revealed Nano Banana Pro and Seedream 4.5 as top proprietary performers, with FLUX.2 [klein] 9B emerging as the leading open-source model.
solution: A comprehensive benchmark, GEditBench v2, was developed with 23 diverse tasks, including a novel 'open-set' category of real-world instructions to evaluate generalization.
PVC-Judge, an open-source, human-aligned pairwise assessment model, was created specifically for evaluating visual consistency, built upon the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct base model.
VCReward-Bench, a meta-benchmark of 3,506 expert-annotated preference pairs, was constructed to rigorously validate the human alignment of visual consistency assessment models.
paper_summary.summary: A new benchmark for general image editing, GEditBench v2, is introduced alongside PVC-Judge, an open-source visual consistency assessment model. This work reveals that proprietary models generally lead performance, with FLUX.2 [klein] 9B being the top open-source contender, and highlights a trade-off between instruction following and visual consistency.

title: GEditBench v2: A Human-Aligned Benchmark for General Image Editing

abstract: 3D editing - the task of locally modifying the geometry or appearance of a 3D asset - has wide applications in immersive content creation, digital entertainment, and AR/VR. However, unlike 2D editing, it remains challenging due to the need for cross-view consistency, structural fidelity, and fine-grained controllability. Existing approaches are often slow, prone to geometric distortions, or dependent on manual and accurate 3D masks that are error-prone and impractical. To address these challenges, we advance both the data and model fronts. On the data side, we introduce 3DEditVerse, the largest paired 3D editing benchmark to date, comprising 116,309 high-quality training pairs and 1,500 curated test pairs. Built through complementary pipelines of pose-driven geometric edits and foundation model-guided appearance edits, 3DEditVerse ensures edit locality, multi-view consistency, and semantic alignment. On the model side, we propose 3DEditFormer, a 3D-structure-preserving conditional transformer. By enhancing image-to-3D generation with dual-guidance attention and time-adaptive gating, 3DEditFormer disentangles editable regions from preserved structure, enabling precise and consistent edits without requiring auxiliary 3D masks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework outperforms state-of-the-art baselines both quantitatively and qualitatively, establishing a new standard for practical and scalable 3D editing. Dataset and code will be released. Project: this https URL

organization_info.name: East China University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: A meticulously curated, large-scale paired dataset using automated pipelines and a 3D lifting strategy that preserves consistency is crucial for overcoming data scarcity and enabling supervised training of advanced 3D editing models.

Integrating multi-stage structural features (fine-grained from late timesteps and semantic transition from early timesteps) via a dual-guidance attention mechanism allows a model to inherently understand and preserve unedited 3D structure without explicit masks.

A time-adaptive gating mechanism that dynamically controls the influence of structural and semantic guidance at different diffusion timesteps is effective in achieving precise, localized edits while maintaining global structural fidelity.
originalProblem: Existing 3D editing methods suffer from slowness, cross-view inconsistency, and geometric distortions, making them impractical for interactive use.
Current approaches often rely on labor-intensive and error-prone manual 3D masks to define editable regions, leading to unintended changes or edits bleeding into unedited areas.
There is a scarcity of large-scale, high-fidelity paired 3D editing datasets required to train robust and generalizable models for localized and consistent 3D modifications.
results: 3DEditFormer consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods like EditP23, Instant3dit, and VoxHammer across both qualitative and quantitative evaluations, producing high-quality, localized, and structure-preserving modifications.

The model achieves a 13% improvement in 3D metrics (Chamfer Distance, Normal Consistency, F1@0.01) compared to VoxHammer, which relies on ground-truth 3D masks, demonstrating superior fidelity and a more practical mask-free workflow.

Ablation studies confirm that both multi-stage structural guidance and the time-adaptive gating mechanism are essential components, contributing significantly to the model's ability to achieve robust geometric fidelity and consistent edits.
solution: A new large-scale, high-fidelity paired 3D editing benchmark, 3DEditVerse, comprising over 116,000 training pairs, is constructed through automated pipelines for both pose-driven geometric and text-guided generative appearance edits, ensuring 3D consistency.

3DEditFormer, a structure-preserving conditional transformer, is developed to perform precise, localized, and mask-free 3D edits by injecting multi-stage structural priors from the source asset into the target generation process.

The model utilizes a Dual-Guidance Attention Block with fine-grained structural features and semantic transition features, alongside a time-adaptive gating mechanism, to dynamically balance structural fidelity and semantic changes across diffusion timesteps.
paper_summary.summary: The research introduces 3DEditVerse, the largest paired 3D editing dataset, and 3DEditFormer, a structure-preserving conditional transformer, to enable precise and consistent 3D edits from text prompts without requiring manual 3D masks. The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating a 13% improvement in 3D metrics over prior methods dependent on ground-truth masks.

title: Towards Scalable and Consistent 3D Editing

abstract: Current instruction-based image editing (IBIE) methods struggle with challenging editing tasks, as both editing types and sample counts of existing datasets are limited. Moreover, traditional dataset construction often contains noisy image-caption pairs, which may introduce biases and limit model capabilities in complex editing scenarios. To address these limitations, we introduce MultiEdit, a comprehensive dataset featuring over 107K high-quality image editing samples. It encompasses 6 challenging editing tasks through a diverse collection of 18 non-style-transfer editing types and 38 style transfer operations, covering a spectrum from sophisticated style transfer to complex semantic operations like person reference editing and in-image text editing. We employ a novel dataset construction pipeline that utilizes two multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) to generate visual-adaptive editing instructions and produce high-fidelity edited images, respectively. Extensive experiments demonstrate that fine-tuning foundational open-source models with our MultiEdit-Train set substantially improves models' performance on sophisticated editing tasks in our proposed MultiEdit-Test benchmark, while effectively preserving their capabilities on the standard editing benchmark. We believe MultiEdit provides a valuable resource for advancing research into more diverse and challenging IBIE capabilities. Our dataset is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Direct MLLM interpretation of source images, guided by meta-instructions, is crucial for generating precise, context-aware editing instructions, overcoming limitations of caption-based methods.
Leveraging powerful generative AI (SOTA MLLMs and ImageGen models) for synthetic data generation can effectively create large-scale, high-quality training data for complex multi-modal tasks.

Data-driven multi-task learning, particularly task-weighted data sampling that up-weights more complex editing categories, is highly effective for training models on heterogeneous instruction-based editing datasets.

Fine-tuning with specialized, high-quality data like MultiEdit not only improves performance on complex tasks but also preserves or enhances capabilities on standard editing benchmarks, demonstrating strong generalization.
originalProblem: Existing datasets for instruction-based image editing (IBIE) are limited in diversity and scale, focusing primarily on simple, single-entity modifications and neglecting complex scenarios.
Current datasets often overlook challenging editing tasks requiring sophisticated spatial or semantic reasoning, such as object/person reference editing, view editing, or GUI manipulation.
Traditional caption-based methods for dataset construction are prone to introducing noise, information loss, and misalignment between instructions and visual content.
State-of-the-art open-source IBIE models often struggle with complex, real-world editing scenarios due to a lack of appropriate training data.
results: Fine-tuning open-source models (SD3 and UltraEdit) with MultiEdit-Train led to substantial performance improvements on challenging tasks, with SD3's CLIPimg increasing by ~9.4% and DINO scores by ~16.1% on MultiEdit-Test.
Models fine-tuned with MultiEdit-Train also preserved or enhanced performance on standard benchmarks like EmuEdit-Test, with SD3's CLIPimg improving by ~8.1%.

Data-driven multi-task learning, specifically an aggressively up-weighted sampling strategy for complex tasks, proved most effective, allowing UltraEdit to achieve CLIPimg scores competitive with SOTA and DINO scores surpassing them by over 5% on MultiEdit-Test.

Qualitative results confirm that fine-tuned models exhibit significantly improved instruction comprehension and precise editing for diverse and complex tasks, outperforming baselines that struggle with specific challenging edits.
solution: Developed MultiEdit, a dataset comprising over 107K high-quality, fine-grained image editing triplets across 6 challenging task categories and 56 editing types.

Introduced an MLLM-driven data construction pipeline that leverages state-of-the-art multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) for visual-adaptive instruction generation and SOTA ImageGen models for high-fidelity image editing.
Implemented a rigorous two-step quality assessment process, integrating expert-designed meta-instructions and human/AI-assisted checks, to ensure data accuracy and fidelity.
Explored various multi-task learning strategies, including data-driven (task-weighted sampling) and loss-driven approaches, to effectively train models on the heterogeneous MultiEdit dataset.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Inclusion AI and collaborating universities developed MultiEdit, a dataset of over 107K high-quality image editing triplets, utilizing an MLLM-driven pipeline to address the scarcity of diverse training data for instruction-based image editing. Fine-tuning open-source models with MultiEdit-Train significantly enhanced their ability to perform complex editing tasks, improving SD3's CLIPimg by approximately 9.4% and DINO by 16.1% on the new benchmark.

title: MultiEdit: Advancing Instruction-based Image Editing on Diverse and Challenging Tasks

abstract: Despite the recent success of multi-view diffusion models for text/image-based 3D asset generation, instruction-based editing of 3D assets lacks surprisingly far behind the quality of generation models. The main reason is that recent approaches using 2D priors suffer from view-inconsistent editing signals. Going beyond 2D prior distillation methods and multi-view editing strategies, we propose a training-free editing method that operates within the latent space of a native 3D diffusion model, allowing us to directly manipulate 3D geometry. We guide the edit synthesis by blending 3D attention maps from the generation with the source object. Coupled with geometry-aware regularization guidance, a spectral modulation strategy in the Fourier domain and a refinement step for 3D enhancement, our method outperforms previous 3D editing methods enabling high-fidelity and precise edits across a wide range of shapes and semantic manipulations. Our project webpage is this https URL

keyInsights: Operating directly within the latent space of a native 3D diffusion model enables true 3D consistency and robust geometric transformations, overcoming limitations of 2D prior-based methods.
3D self- and cross-attention maps within the diffusion process inherently capture spatial and semantic relationships, making them effective for guiding instruction-based 3D edits.
A combination of localized masking, geometry regularization to prevent degradation, frequency annealing for texture quality, and 3D enhancement is vital for high-fidelity and plausible 3D results.
originalProblem: Existing text-guided 3D editing methods struggle with "multi-view inconsistencies" and lack true 3D awareness, leading to artifacts and compromised 3D integrity.
Many approaches are limited to superficial appearance changes, failing to perform robust geometric or structural transformations of 3D objects.
Current techniques often produce blurry, distorted, or implausible 3D reconstructions due to the propagation of inconsistencies from 2D priors or multi-view synthesis.
results: Achieved the highest CLIP-directional similarity (0.178) and multi-view consistency (CLIP-Dir-Con 0.77), outperforming existing baselines in adherence to edit instructions and 3D consistency.
Showed superior performance in GPTEval3D, with a 94% win rate against baselines for text-prompt to 3D alignment and an 83% win rate for 3D plausibility.
A user study found 3D-LATTE preferred by 83.2% of participants for instruction faithfulness and 74.0% for visual quality, significantly surpassing competitors.
solution: A training-free framework operates directly within the latent space of DiffSplat, a native 3D diffusion model, to enable 3D-consistent editing.
3D attention injection is employed, blending cross-attention for semantic alignment and injecting self-attention from the source to preserve structural relationships.
Localized editing is achieved through VLM-guided 3D masking, augmented by geometry regularization, frequency annealing, and an iterative 3D enhancement pipeline.
paper_summary.summary: 3D-LATTE enables high-fidelity, geometrically consistent editing of 3D objects using textual instructions by operating directly within the latent space of a native 3D diffusion model. The method significantly outperforms existing baselines, achieving a 94% win rate in text-3D alignment and an 83.2% user preference for instruction faithfulness.

title: 3D-LATTE: Latent Space 3D Editing from Textual Instructions

abstract: We introduce "ImageDream," an innovative image-prompt, multi-view diffusion model for 3D object generation. ImageDream stands out for its ability to produce 3D models of higher quality compared to existing state-of-the-art, image-conditioned methods. Our approach utilizes a canonical camera coordination for the objects in images, improving visual geometry accuracy. The model is designed with various levels of control at each block inside the diffusion model based on the input image, where global control shapes the overall object layout and local control fine-tunes the image details. The effectiveness of ImageDream is demonstrated through extensive evaluations using a standard prompt list. For more information, visit our project page at this https URL.
title: ImageDream: Image-Prompt Multi-view Diffusion for 3D Generation

abstract: Visually-guided image editing, where edits are conditioned on both visual cues and textual prompts, has emerged as a powerful paradigm for fine-grained, controllable content generation. Although recent generative models have shown remarkable capabilities, existing evaluations remain simple and insufficiently representative of real-world editing challenges. We present SpotEdit, a comprehensive benchmark designed to systematically assess visually-guided image editing methods across diverse diffusion, autoregressive, and hybrid generative models, uncovering substantial performance disparities. To address a critical yet underexplored challenge, our benchmark includes a dedicated component on hallucination, highlighting how leading models, such as GPT-4o, often hallucinate the existence of a visual cue and erroneously perform the editing task. Our code and benchmark are publicly released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Visually-guided image editing remains fundamentally challenging for state-of-the-art models, which often exhibit trade-offs between accurately replicating target objects and preserving background consistency.

Generative models, including proprietary solutions like GPT-4o, are highly vulnerable to 'hallucination,' frequently introducing unintended modifications or erroneous objects when target cues are absent.
The complexity of real-world video frames consistently poses greater challenges to models compared to synthetic data, highlighting the need for better generalization to diverse visual contexts.
originalProblem: Existing benchmarks for visually-guided image editing are too simple, often focusing on coarse manipulations or single-object scenes, failing to capture real-world complexities.
Text-only image editing approaches lack fine-grained control and consistent semantic alignment, prompting the need for visually-guided methods.

There is an underdeveloped methodology for rigorously evaluating critical failure modes like 'hallucination' in generative image editing models, where models erroneously create objects or edits when instructed not to.
results: BAGEL and OmniGen2 emerged as the strongest performers in standard editing, with BAGEL excelling in background preservation and OmniGen2 achieving higher object fidelity.

All evaluated models, including GPT-4o, showed 'alarmingly high' failure rates in hallucination scenarios; GPT-4o was particularly vulnerable with failure rates exceeding 80-90% for erroneous object insertions.
BAGEL demonstrated superior robustness in the hallucination setting, outperforming other models on 6 out of 8 metrics, with significantly lower failure rates compared to GPT-4o.
solution: SpotEdit provides a novel, comprehensive benchmark with 500 samples, including a 'Standard Setting' for diverse editing tasks and a unique 'Hallucination Setting' to specifically test model robustness to missing visual cues.

A three-stage data generation pipeline, leveraging LLMs like Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, InternVL3-8B, and GPT-4o, creates fine-grained object-level editing instructions and ground-truth edited frames from synthetic and real-world video sources.

A multi-faceted evaluation strategy utilizes metrics for Global Score, Object Fidelity, Background Fidelity, and a novel Failure Rate for hallucination, enabling precise quantitative assessment of open and closed-source models.
paper_summary.summary: SpotEdit introduces a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate visually-guided image editing methods, rigorously assessing model performance across diverse editing tasks and their robustness to hallucination. The evaluation revealed that state-of-the-art models face significant challenges with object fidelity and background preservation, and exhibit alarmingly high rates of erroneously adding objects in hallucination scenarios.

title: SpotEdit: Evaluating Visually-Guided Image Editing Methods

abstract: Interactive point-based image editing serves as a controllable editor, enabling precise and flexible manipulation of image content. However, most drag-based methods operate primarily on the 2D pixel plane with limited use of 3D cues. As a result, they often produce imprecise and inconsistent edits, particularly in geometry-intensive scenarios such as rotations and perspective transformations. To address these limitations, we propose a novel geometry-guided drag-based image editing method-GeoDrag, which addresses three key challenges: 1) incorporating 3D geometric cues into pixel-level editing, 2) mitigating discontinuities caused by geometry-only guidance, and 3) resolving conflicts arising from multi-point dragging. Built upon a unified displacement field that jointly encodes 3D geometry and 2D spatial priors, GeoDrag enables coherent, high-fidelity, and structure-consistent editing in a single forward pass. In addition, a conflict-free partitioning strategy is introduced to isolate editing regions, effectively preventing interference and ensuring consistency. Extensive experiments across various editing scenarios validate the effectiveness of our method, showing superior precision, structural consistency, and reliable multi-point editability. Project page: this https URL.
title: Dragging with Geometry: From Pixels to Geometry-Guided Image Editing

abstract: Instruction-guided 3D editing is a rapidly emerging field with the potential to broaden access to 3D content creation. However, existing methods face critical limitations: optimization-based approaches are prohibitively slow, while feed-forward approaches relying on multi-view 2D editing often suffer from inconsistent geometry and degraded visual quality. To address these issues, we propose a novel native 3D editing framework that directly manipulates 3D representations in a single, efficient feed-forward pass. Specifically, we create a large-scale, multi-modal dataset for instruction-guided 3D editing, covering diverse addition, deletion, and modification tasks. This dataset is meticulously curated to ensure that edited objects faithfully adhere to the instructional changes while preserving the consistency of unedited regions with the source object. Building upon this dataset, we explore two distinct conditioning strategies for our model: a conventional cross-attention mechanism and a novel 3D token concatenation approach. Our results demonstrate that token concatenation is more parameter-efficient and achieves superior performance. Extensive evaluations show that our method outperforms existing 2D-lifting approaches, setting a new benchmark in generation quality, 3D consistency, and instruction fidelity.

keyInsights: Direct manipulation of 3D representations (native 3D editing) in a feed-forward pass leads to superior 3D consistency and visual fidelity compared to 2D-lifting methods.
The 3D Token Concatenation strategy for conditioning generative 3D models significantly outperforms traditional cross-attention in maintaining object coherence and precise editing.
Meticulously curated, high-fidelity datasets are crucial for training robust instruction-guided 3D editing models, especially when relying on 2D-to-3D lifting for data generation.
originalProblem: Optimization-based 3D editing methods are computationally intensive and slow, hindering rapid iteration.
Feed-forward 2D-lifting approaches often result in inconsistent 3D geometry and degraded visual quality due to multi-view misalignment.
A significant lack of large-scale, high-quality datasets specifically designed for instruction-guided native 3D editing exists.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art quantitative performance with FID of 91.9, FVD of 286.5, and CLIP score of 0.249, surpassing existing methods.
Enabled efficient, single-pass instruction-guided 3D editing, generating modified 3D assets within 20 seconds per edit.

Qualitative results demonstrate precise instruction-following while meticulously preserving the original appearance and structural coherence of unedited regions, addressing inconsistencies of prior methods.
solution: Developed a "native 3D editing" framework that directly manipulates 3D representations based on text instructions in an efficient feed-forward manner.
Constructed a large-scale, multi-modal dataset for 3D editing tasks (deletion, addition, modification) using Objaverse, Gemini 2.5, 3D-Alpaca, and Hunyuan3D 2.1 with rigorous manual curation.

Proposed a novel and parameter-efficient "3D Token Concatenation" conditioning strategy, where source and target 3D latents are concatenated to allow self-attention to model their relationships directly.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Fudan University, StepFun, Inc., and collaborators introduced a "native 3D editing" framework that performs instruction-guided 3D object modifications in a single, feed-forward pass, circumventing issues of 2D-lifting methods. This approach, powered by a new large-scale dataset and a 3D token concatenation conditioning strategy, generates high-quality edited 3D assets within 20 seconds and achieves state-of-the-art FID, FVD, and CLIP scores.

title: Native 3D Editing with Full Attention

abstract:
Scene image editing is crucial for entertainment, photography, and
advertising design. Existing methods solely focus on either 2D individual
object or 3D global scene editing. This results in a lack of a unified approach
to effectively control and manipulate scenes at the 3D level with different
levels of granularity. In this work, we propose 3DitScene, a novel and unified
scene editing framework leveraging language-guided disentangled Gaussian
Splatting that enables seamless editing from 2D to 3D, allowing precise control
over scene composition and individual objects. We first incorporate 3D
Gaussians that are refined through generative priors and optimization
techniques. Language features from CLIP then introduce semantics into 3D
geometry for object disentanglement. With the disentangled Gaussians, 3DitScene
allows for manipulation at both the global and individual levels,
revolutionizing creative expression and empowering control over scenes and
objects. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and versatility of
3DitScene in scene image editing. Code and online demo can be found at our
project homepage: https://zqh0253.github.io/3DitScene/.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Combining 3D Gaussian Splatting with language models and diffusion models enables robust 3D scene representation and editing from a single image.
Disentangling 3D objects through semantic feature distillation and layout augmentation allows for precise, language-guided object-level manipulation.
Expanding the 3DGS representation with depth-aware inpainting helps generate consistent novel views, even for previously unobserved regions of the scene.
originalProblem: Existing 2D image editing methods lack 3D consistency and camera control for scene manipulation.
Current 3D scene editing techniques often operate globally, with limited ability to manipulate individual objects independently.
Single-view 3D scene synthesis struggles to produce high texture quality and maintain viewpoint consistency across different camera angles.
results: The system qualitatively demonstrates precise object manipulation (translation, rotation, removal) and scene re-stylization, maintaining 3D consistency and proper occlusion relationships.
Ablation studies confirm that layout augmentation significantly improves disentanglement quality and occlusion handling, while both SDS and reconstruction losses are essential for image quality.
User studies show 3DitScene consistently outperforms baseline methods in consistency with the original image and overall image quality.
solution: Initializes a 3D Gaussian Splatting representation from a single input image using estimated depth and refines it with reconstruction and Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) losses.
Expands the 3DGS for novel views by inpainting uncovered regions with Stable Diffusion and estimating new depth, then projecting these pixels into the 3D Gaussian representation.
Achieves language-guided disentanglement by integrating CLIP language features into 3D Gaussians, using SAM for segmentation, and applying layout augmentation during training.
paper_summary.summary: 3DitScene introduces a framework for language-guided 3D scene editing from a single input image, enabling object-level manipulation with 3D consistency. The system combines disentangled 3D Gaussian Splatting with diffusion models and language models, demonstrating the ability to edit object appearance and layout while preserving scene coherence.

title:
3DitScene: Editing Any Scene via Language-guided Disentangled Gaussian
  Splatting

abstract: Diffusion Handles is a novel approach to enabling 3D object edits on diffusion images. We accomplish these edits using existing pre-trained diffusion models, and 2D image depth estimation, without any fine-tuning or 3D object retrieval. The edited results remain plausible, photo-real, and preserve object identity. Diffusion Handles address a critically missing facet of generative image based creative design, and significantly advance the state-of-the-art in generative image editing. Our key insight is to lift diffusion activations for an object to 3D using a proxy depth, 3D-transform the depth and associated activations, and project them back to image space. The diffusion process applied to the manipulated activations with identity control, produces plausible edited images showing complex 3D occlusion and lighting effects. We evaluate Diffusion Handles: quantitatively, on a large synthetic data benchmark; and qualitatively by a user study, showing our output to be more plausible, and better than prior art at both, 3D editing and identity control. Project Webpage: this https URL
title: Diffusion Handles: Enabling 3D Edits for Diffusion Models by Lifting Activations to 3D

abstract: Existing 2D-lifting-based 3D editing methods often encounter challenges related to inconsistency, stemming from the lack of view-consistent 2D editing models and the difficulty of ensuring consistent editing across multiple views. To address these issues, we propose C3Editor, a controllable and consistent 2D-lifting-based 3D editing framework. Given an original 3D representation and a text-based editing prompt, our method selectively establishes a view-consistent 2D editing model to achieve superior 3D editing results. The process begins with the controlled selection of a ground truth (GT) view and its corresponding edited image as the optimization target, allowing for user-defined manual edits. Next, we fine-tune the 2D editing model within the GT view and across multiple views to align with the GT-edited image while ensuring multi-view consistency. To meet the distinct requirements of GT view fitting and multi-view consistency, we introduce separate LoRA modules for targeted fine-tuning. Our approach delivers more consistent and controllable 2D and 3D editing results than existing 2D-lifting-based methods, outperforming them in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations.
title: C3Editor: Achieving Controllable Consistency in 2D Model for 3D Editing

abstract:
The success of image generative models has enabled us to build methods that
can edit images based on text or other user input. However, these methods are
bespoke, imprecise, require additional information, or are limited to only 2D
image edits. We present GeoDiffuser, a zero-shot optimization-based method that
unifies common 2D and 3D image-based object editing capabilities into a single
method. Our key insight is to view image editing operations as geometric
transformations. We show that these transformations can be directly
incorporated into the attention layers in diffusion models to implicitly
perform editing operations. Our training-free optimization method uses an
objective function that seeks to preserve object style but generate plausible
images, for instance with accurate lighting and shadows. It also inpaints
disoccluded parts of the image where the object was originally located. Given a
natural image and user input, we segment the foreground object using SAM and
estimate a corresponding transform which is used by our optimization approach
for editing. GeoDiffuser can perform common 2D and 3D edits like object
translation, 3D rotation, and removal. We present quantitative results,
including a perceptual study, that shows how our approach is better than
existing methods. Visit this https URL for
more information.

keyInsights: Geometric transformations can be effectively integrated into the attention mechanism of diffusion models by transforming reference queries before attention computation.

Combining a reference diffusion process (for style preservation) with an edit diffusion process (for new content generation) via masked blending allows for realistic object transformation and background inpainting.
A zero-shot, optimization-based approach, guided by specific loss terms, can achieve precise and realistic geometric edits without requiring additional model training or fine-tuning.
originalProblem: Text-guided diffusion models lack precise geometric control for object manipulation (e.g., exact translations, rotations).
Interactive point/stroke-based editing methods often struggle with large-scale geometric transformations, accurate disoccluded background inpainting, or preserving object identity.
Existing 3D-aware editing approaches typically require multi-view inputs or specific 3D model training, limiting their application to single natural images.
results: Achieved unified editing for 2D translation/scaling, 3D rotation/translation/scaling, and object removal, maintaining high-quality realism, style consistency, and accurate disoccluded background inpainting.
Outperformed state-of-the-art baselines in edit adherence (Mean Distance 5.579 for 2D edits, 7.304 for 3D edits) and maintained high content preservation (CLIP Similarity above 0.960).
A perceptual study showed a strong preference for GeoDiffuser's inpainting (94.06%) and geometric edits (86.48% for realism, 88.48% for adherence) over compared methods.
solution: Performs DDIM inversion on the input image to get a noise latent trajectory, then guides an "edit diffusion process" with geometric transformations.
Bakes geometric transformations (derived from 2D coordinates or 3D depth maps) directly into the attention layers of a pre-trained diffusion model.
Optimizes latent representations and null-initialized text embeddings with a loss function that includes background preservation, object placement, and an inpainting loss to fill disoccluded regions.
paper_summary.summary: GeoDiffuser introduces a zero-shot, optimization-based method for precise geometric image editing, enabling 2D and 3D object transformations and removal within a single framework. It quantitatively outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in edit adherence (e.g., Mean Distance 5.579 for 2D edits vs. 38.070 for Dragon Diffusion) and maintains high content preservation.

title: GeoDiffuser: Geometry-Based Image Editing with Diffusion Models

abstract: 3D editing has emerged as a critical research area to provide users with flexible control over 3D assets. While current editing approaches predominantly focus on 3D Gaussian Splatting or multi-view images, the direct editing of 3D meshes remains underexplored. Prior attempts, such as VoxHammer, rely on voxel-based representations that suffer from limited resolution and necessitate labor-intensive 3D mask. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{VecSet-Edit}, the first pipeline that leverages the high-fidelity VecSet Large Reconstruction Model (LRM) as a backbone for mesh editing. Our approach is grounded on a analysis of the spatial properties in VecSet tokens, revealing that token subsets govern distinct geometric regions. Based on this insight, we introduce Mask-guided Token Seeding and Attention-aligned Token Gating strategies to precisely localize target regions using only 2D image conditions. Also, considering the difference between VecSet diffusion process versus voxel we design a Drift-aware Token Pruning to reject geometric outliers during the denoising process. Finally, our Detail-preserving Texture Baking module ensures that we not only preserve the geometric details of original mesh but also the textural information. More details can be found in our project page: this https URL

keyInsights: VecSet tokens, despite being an unordered set, maintain stable locality and consistently correspond to specific surface regions, enabling targeted manipulation.
Precise 3D region localization for editing can be achieved through lightweight 2D image and mask guidance by leveraging cross-attention and self-attention maps within VecSet Diffusion Transformers.
Mitigating token drift during diffusion and applying geometry-aware texture baking are critical for maintaining clean edit boundaries and preserving the fidelity of unedited mesh regions.
originalProblem: Generating usable 3D assets often requires extensive post-processing and fine-tuning, especially for localized and controllable edits.

Existing 3D editing methods operating on implicit representations (NeRFs, 3DGS) do not directly yield explicit mesh topology, while voxel-based LRMs suffer from resolution limitations and require labor-intensive 3D mask annotations.

VecSet-based Large Reconstruction Models (LRMs), despite their high fidelity, present a challenge for localized editing due to their unordered token structure, making it difficult to map 2D edit regions to specific 3D tokens.
results: VecSet-Edit achieved a Chamfer Distance of 0.011 on unedited regions, demonstrating superior preservation quality with a 21% improvement over the previous state-of-the-art (VoxHammer at 0.014).
The framework processed edits efficiently in approximately 200 seconds, marking a 2x speedup compared to voxel-based LRMs like Trellis and VoxHammer.

Qualitative evaluations confirm superior detail preservation in unedited regions, accurate reflection of semantic changes from 2D image conditions, and high-fidelity texture maintenance through its detail-preserving baking module.
solution: VecSet-Edit introduces a training-free framework that decomposes the latent VecSet into editable and preservation subsets based on a novel 'VecSet Geometry Property', which empirically shows that subsets of VecSet tokens correspond to coherent surface regions.
It employs a two-stage token selection mechanism (Mask-guided Token Seeding and Attention-aligned Token Gating) to precisely identify 3D tokens corresponding to a 2D edit mask.

The framework adapts the RePaint strategy to the VecSet latent space, incorporating Drift-aware Token Pruning to prevent geometric overlap and Detail-preserving Texture Baking to maintain high-frequency texture details in unedited regions.
paper_summary.summary: VecSet-Edit introduces a training-free framework for localized 3D mesh editing from a single 2D image, leveraging pre-trained VecSet Large Reconstruction Models. The method achieves superior preservation of unedited regions with a Chamfer Distance of 0.011, representing a 21% improvement over prior state-of-the-art, and offers a 2x speedup in processing time.

title: VecSet-Edit: Unleashing Pre-trained LRM for Mesh Editing from Single Image

abstract: Vision-centric autonomous driving systems require diverse data for robust training and evaluation, which can be augmented by manipulating object positions and appearances within existing scene captures. While recent advancements in diffusion models have shown promise in video editing, their application to object manipulation in driving scenarios remains challenging due to imprecise positional control and difficulties in preserving high-fidelity object appearances. To address these challenges in position and appearance control, we introduce DriveEditor, a diffusion-based framework for object editing in driving videos. DriveEditor offers a unified framework for comprehensive object editing operations, including repositioning, replacement, deletion, and insertion. These diverse manipulations are all achieved through a shared set of varying inputs, processed by identical position control and appearance maintenance modules. The position control module projects the given 3D bounding box while preserving depth information and hierarchically injects it into the diffusion process, enabling precise control over object position and orientation. The appearance maintenance module preserves consistent attributes with a single reference image by employing a three-tiered approach: low-level detail preservation, high-level semantic maintenance, and the integration of 3D priors from a novel view synthesis model. Extensive qualitative and quantitative evaluations on the nuScenes dataset demonstrate DriveEditor's exceptional fidelity and controllability in generating diverse driving scene edits, as well as its remarkable ability to facilitate downstream tasks. Project page: this https URL.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Integrating depth information from 3D bounding box projections into a diffusion model significantly enhances precise 3D positional and orientational control of objects.

A multi-tiered approach to appearance control, combining both direct image data and extracted semantic features with 3D prior information, is crucial for maintaining object fidelity and consistency across video frames.
A two-stage training strategy effectively balances learning robust position control from bounding boxes with the benefits of 3D information fusion for improved appearance and consistency.
originalProblem: Autonomous driving datasets suffer from long-tailed distributions, making rare but critical scenarios scarce and costly to acquire or annotate.
Existing generative models for driving scenes lack fine-grained control over individual object appearance and precise 3D positioning/orientation.
General video editing methods struggle with accurate 3D positional control and maintaining consistent object appearance from a single reference image in dynamic environments.
results: DriveEditor achieves superior editing quality, with FID scores of 30.17 for deletion (compared to 36.13 for ProPainter) and 11.83 for replacement (compared to 14.22 for Text2Video-Zero), demonstrating more realistic and temporally coherent results.
